coredata.dc:title: Enhanced oxygen evolution reaction performance of nitrogen-doped carbon dots sensitized with rare-earth metal nanorods
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Selvaraju, Nithya
Sasi, Sheethal
Sivalingam, Yuvaraj
Venugopal, Gunasekaran
coredata.dc:description: An affordable, sustainable, effective, and strong catalyst for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) processes has gained increased significance because of its crucial role in energy conversion and storage. Heteroatom-doped amorphous carbon materials made from biomass have lately come into prominence as inexpensive, environmentally benign, renewable, and sustainable materials. Substantial research about the incredible performance in OER by carbon dots has been devoted, yet durability remains a challenge. Therefore, we reported novel carbon dots doped with a heteroatom (nCDs) included with Gd(OH)3, a rare-earth hydroxide element. Taking advantage of numerous active and edge sites of CDs and modifying the chemistry of the surface by doping with foreign atoms, the prepared nCDs/Gd(OH)3 revealed the outstanding catalytic performance of a low overpotential of 280 mV at a Tafel slope of 127 mV/dec at 10 mA/cm2 in sluggish alkaline medium. Our findings supported the synergistic interaction between the doped heteroatom and its surface's maximal exposure of nCDs/Gd(OH)3 sites, facilitating the rapid transport of reactants and sufficient contact to enhance OER performance. This study will garner more attention in the context of producing sustainable energy and storing it effectively. For clean, green energy, combining carbon dots with rare earth metals is an emerging candidate for energy conversion. The alkaline electrolyzer will find it to be a promising electrocatalyst, especially in seawater medium.
coredata.dcterms:subject: nCDs
Oxygen evolution reaction
Electrolysis
Gadolinium

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nCDs from Bhringraj leaves extract
Synergetic impact of nCDs incorporated with Gd(OH)3 nanorods

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of nCDs [Inset: SAED pattern (a)], HRTEM images of nCDs/Gd(OH)3 at different magnification (b, c [Inset: SAED pattern(c)], d) lattice fringes of nCDs and nCDs/Gd(OH)3 (e,f).
(a) XRD pattern (b) FTIR of nCDs/Gd(OH)3, nCDs, and Gd(OH)3 and (c) Raman spectra of nCDs/Gd(OH)3.
(a) XPS survey spectra of nCDs/Gd(OH)3 [Inset: elemental percentage], (b) De-convoluted C 1 s spectra, (c) N 1 s spectra, (d) O 1 s spectra, and (e) Gd 4d spectra.
(a) LSV spectra, (b) Comparative graph, (c) Tafel slope of bare GCE, nCDs, and nCDs/Gd(OH)3, and (d) Stability test by Chrono-potentiometry @ 10 mA/cm2.
(a) Nyquist plots and (b) Bode plots of nCDs and nCDs/Gd(OH)3, (c) CV curves at different scan rates, and (b) Current density vs scan rate.
(a) HRTEM image of nCDs/Gd(OH)3, (b) Comparative graph, (c) SKP raster scanning images, (d) Time constant of nCDs and nCDs/Gd(OH)3.
Schematic diagram of energy levels of nCDs and Gd(OH)3 nanorod.
Comparison of OER performance of carbon quantum dots/composites.

head.ce:title: Enhanced oxygen evolution reaction performance of nitrogen-doped carbon dots sensitized with rare-earth metal nanorods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Nanomaterials and System Lab, Department of Materials Science, School of Technology, Central University of Tamil Nadu, Thiruvarur – 610 005, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Nanomaterials and System Lab, Department of Materials Science, School of Technology, Central University of Tamil Nadu, Thiruvarur – 610 005, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory for Sensors, Energy and Electronic Devices, Department of Physics and Nanotechnology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Chennai- 603203, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory for Sensors, Energy and Electronic Devices
SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory for Sensors, Energy and Electronic Devices, Department of Physics and Nanotechnology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Chennai- 603203, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An affordable, sustainable, effective, and strong catalyst for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) processes has gained increased significance because of its crucial role in energy conversion and storage. Heteroatom-doped amorphous carbon materials made from biomass have lately come into prominence as inexpensive, environmentally benign, renewable, and sustainable materials. Substantial research about the incredible performance in OER by carbon dots has been devoted, yet durability remains a challenge. Therefore, we reported novel carbon dots doped with a heteroatom (nCDs) included with Gd(OH)3, a rare-earth hydroxide element. Taking advantage of numerous active and edge sites of CDs and modifying the chemistry of the surface by doping with foreign atoms, the prepared nCDs/Gd(OH)3 revealed the outstanding catalytic performance of a low overpotential of 280 mV at a Tafel slope of 127 mV/dec at 10 mA/cm2 in sluggish alkaline medium. Our findings supported the synergistic interaction between the doped heteroatom and its surface's maximal exposure of nCDs/Gd(OH)3 sites, facilitating the rapid transport of reactants and sufficient contact to enhance OER performance. This study will garner more attention in the context of producing sustainable energy and storing it effectively. For clean, green energy, combining carbon dots with rare earth metals is an emerging candidate for energy conversion. The alkaline electrolyzer will find it to be a promising electrocatalyst, especially in seawater medium.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The key to tackling the climate change crisis is to end our reliance on energy generated from fossil fuels. Interest in the field of production and conversion has skyrocketed due to the global demand for new energy sources. Electrolysis in alkaline water is an important approach for sustainable and environmentally friendly energy conversion technologies, including water splitting and rechargeable metal-air batteries. In the electrolysis of water, a 4e- transfer reaction occurs in the anode part, which is known as the oxygen evolution reaction. It is a lethargic kinetics reaction that hampers the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) at the cathode and raises the overpotential [1]. Due to the high thermodynamic energy barrier during the production of the OO bond and the rupture of the OH bond, the evolution of the oxygen process is assumed to be the rate-limiting phase. Therefore, accelerating this process will significantly improve the effectiveness of water-splitting [2,3]. In order to achieve the lower potential, efficient electrocatalyst noble metals are used, which are highly expensive and challenging to spend during mass production [4].

For sustainable energy applications and large-scale production, carbon-based electrocatalyst contributes significantly to high performance. Carbon-based materials like fullerene, carbon nanotubes, graphene, and carbon dots have multiple advantageous features, such as abundant available natural sources, reconfigurable molecular structures, and excellent electronic conductivity. In that, nano-sized carbon dots are zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterial that exhibits better durability, excellent photostability, eco-compatibility, and significant electrocatalytic efficiency. These specific features give nanocarbons the ability to perform a variety of applications and firmly pair with other catalytically active components, producing noticeably improved performances [5]. Larger surface area and low-dimensional properties are the other added advantages of carbon quantum dots. In addition to creating new carbon structures, improved hybrid material qualities are obtained through carbon doping, particularly with S and N. Doping of the nitrogen is the sole responsibility for the higher density of manifested active adsorption sites by the conversion of nearby carbon atoms into active sites results in the improvement of electrocatalytic efficiency. Nitrogen-containing segments could easily attach and favor the composites with numerous active sites, and it also prevents corrosion [6].

Recently, scientists have drawn attention to rare earth (RE) metals as a way to expedite electrolysis reactions. Modifying the electrical structure with foreign materials may be an effective strategy for overcoming the aforementioned issues. The addition of a foreign chemical can alter how they conduct electricity, increasing their intrinsic conductivity. Meanwhile, modifying the electronic structure can efficiently regulate the adsorption energy that occurs between active sites and intermediates, increasing electrocatalytic activity. In order to optimize the structure, it is crucial to elect the right species [7]. Moreover, in the host material, the oxygen vacancy can be enhanced by inserting rare earth elements because of the inherent oxophilicity and flexible valence of RE elements that optimize the adsorption energy on the catalyst surface. Specific 4f orbital occupancy and electronic modulation mechanism give a breakthrough for the bottleneck problem of OER kinetics [8]. Almost all the rare earth elements form stable trivalent compounds because of their +3 oxidation state. The incorporation of rare earth elements will form a passive protective RE oxide layer. It possesses more negative enthalpy information, which enhances anticorrosion property and operation stability. Crystalline nature and defects are often available in RE. Gadolinium with hydroxide acts as a better co-catalyst by providing more free flow of electrons and helps in the dissociation of water molecules [9,10].

By improving the oxygen evolution reaction, which is a core element of energy conversion and energy storage systems like regenerative metal-air batteries, electrolyzers, and rechargeable fuel cells, this work will benefit energy storage technology and energy conversion. In this study, gadolinium hydroxide of nanorods was composited with nitrogen-doped carbon dots. As per the earlier studies, one-dimensional nanorods have the advantage of high electron-hole separation, providing plentiful exposed active sites that aid the kinetics of the reaction [11]. Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared from the Wedelia chinensis leaf extract (Bhringraj, Manjal Karisalangani in Tamil) and were taken as a carbon source and ammonium hydroxide as a precursor. Bhringraj is a medicinal plant from the Asteraceae family where the leaf extract is used for dyeing the hair and stimulating the growth of hair. Cephalalgia and skin diseases are cured by treating them with W. chinensis leaf extract. Numerous studies have revealed the presence of phytosteroids, diterpenes, and triterpene saponins in the leaf extract. According to reports, W. chinensis has anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidative, analgesic, antistress, antimicrobial, CNS depressant, anti-osteoporotic, anticonvulsant, hepatoprotective, wound-healing, sedative, anticancer activities [12]. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (nCDs) and gadolinium hydroxide [Gd(OH)3] are synthesized using the conventional hydrothermal technique. Both were composited using the Reflux method, denoted as nCDs/Gd(OH)3. To analyze it, standard laboratory techniques were carried out, and the kinetics of OER were determined.

Leaves of Wedelia chinensis have been purchased from the domestic market in Thiruvarur, Tamil Nadu, India. Aqueous ammonia solution, Potassium hydroxide (KOH), Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH), and Gadolinium nitrate hexahydrate were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Double-distilled (DD) water was employed to create electrolyte solutions. All the electrodes were supplied by Sinsil International Pvt. Ltd., Bengaluru, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nCDs from Bhringraj leaves extract
ce:section.ce:para: DD water was used to wash the leaves, and the juice was squeezed with the help of mortar and pistol and was filtered—the filtered juice and aqua.NH3 of 50 ml was placed in an autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. A liquid of brown-colored solution was obtained and filtered through a PVDF 0.22 μm pore size membrane, and it was stored in the refrigerator for further use.

Gadolinium nitrate of 2.256 g and 15 ml of 2.5 M NaOH were mixed in 30 ml of DD water and continuously stirred for 30 min, following a 24-h hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C. The synthesized material was centrifuged and washed with ethanol and water before being dried at 60 °C overnight.

40 ml of an aqueous nCDs solution and 3 mg of Gd(OH)3 were subjected to the reflux technique in an oil bath at 100 °C for 8 h while being stirred continuously. It was centrifuged, rinsed, and dried. Fig. S2 clearly depicts the synthesis process in Supplementary Information.

XPS analyzer (Model: PHI5000 Version Probe III) was used to determine the electronic states and elemental composition of nCDs/Gd(OH)3. XRD diffraction patterns were evaluated with the help of an X-ray diffractometer (Model: X'Pert PAN, Analytical).
Morphology, Functional groups, and Surface potential were examined by HRTEM (Model: JEM 2100), Perkin Elmer FT-IR spectrophotometer, and Pan analytical zeta potential, respectively.

Scanning Kelvin Probe. (Model: SKP5050 + SPV, KP Technology Ltd., UK), and UV–Vis spectroscopy (Model: Cary-60, Agilent technologies), and an Electrochemical workstation were carried out to study the Work function, energy levels, and oxidation process.

The electrochemical workstation (Model: CHI660E) equipped with a conventional three-electrode system was utilized to examine the catalytic activity of nCDs/Gd(OH)3 in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte with the help of linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), electrochemical impedance studies (EIS), and cyclic voltammetry (CV) techniques. Plain glassy carbon electrodes (GCE) or material-coated GCE, Ag/AgCl, and Pt wire were used as working, reference, and counter electrodes, respectively. To prepare a homogeneous ink for the catalyst, 10 mg of the synthesized compound was dissolved in a combined solvent of 0.8 ml of water and 0.2 ml of ethanol and mixed with 20 μl of Nafion (5 %) solution. Using the drop-casting process, 4 μl of the produced ink was coated on the working electrode with a mass of 0.05 mg. [13].

LSV was carefully assessed between 0 and 1 V at a scan rate of 10 mV/s in O2 saturated electrolyte. The equation below was used to express all potentials in the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) without any IR adjustment.
(1)
E
vs
RHE
=
E
vs
Ag / AgCl
+
E
Ag / AgCl
ref
+ 0.0591 V × pH
E
Ag / AgCl
ref
= 0.1976
V

With the help of LSV graphs, the Tafel slope was estimated using the following equations.
(2)
η = a + b log
j
(3)
η =
E RHE
− 1.23
V
Whereas Ƞ represents over potential, b and j are Tafel slope and current density, respectively.
Impedance studies were taken between 105 and 0.1 Hz frequency ranges with an amplitude of 5 mV at their open circuit potential, and the results were fitted with a suitable equivalent circuit.
To assess the stability and durability of the synthesized material, a chronopotentiometry test at a constant current density of 10 mA/cm2 was performed.

The cyclic voltammetry technique was used to calculate the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) of the prepared material. Through the use of the Cdl, the active surface area ECSA was calculated. Using different scan speeds between 10 and 100 mV/s, CV measurements were taken in the non-Faradaic zone when the electrode was polarised of potential range. The slope of the CV curve was utilized to calculate the capacitance of a double layer. The equation for Cdl in terms of current density is given below, along with the ECSA calculation.
(4)
j dl
= υ
C dl
(5)
ECSA =
C dl
/
C S
Where Cs is the specific capacitance of an identical electrolytic solution.

Using the conventional hydrothermal technique, the leaves extract is converted into brown nCDs solutions and filtered using a dialysis bag. Gd(OH)3 is doped with nCDs using the reflux technique and transformed into black powder after drying.

The surface morphology of well-formed nCDs and nCDs/Gd(OH)3 were studied using HRTEM. Fig. 1(a) illustrates the presence of highly uniformly disseminated and spherical nCDs. The amorphous nature of nCDs was clearly revealed by the SAED pattern shown as an inset in Fig. 1(a) [14]. The histogram reveals the particle size distribution and the average value of the diameter of dots is approximately 2.6 nm, and they ranged from 1 to 5 nm, as shown in the supplementary information. Fig. 1(b, d) depicts the presence of Gd(OH)3 in rod shape maximum and clusters of rods in spherical shape. The synthesized material exhibits all dimensional advantages like one dimension's stability, zero dimension's surface to volume ratio, and energy transfer ability, enhancing the adsorption sites for oxygen evolution. Fig. 1 (e, f) reveals the inter-planar distance between the planes is 0.31 nm, which was depicted by the lattice fringes in the graphitic (002) plane, confirming the amorphous nature and the carbon dots are enriched evenly on the surface of the rods implying that nCDs with enormous functional and are interconnected with that Gd(OH)3. This was confirmed by the SAED pattern as inset (in Fig. 1c) of nCDs/Gd(OH)3 where the nature of carbon dots was changed from amorphous into partial crystalline nature in composite [15].

Fig. 2 represents the XRD pattern and aids in obtaining needed information regarding the presence of elements and information on the crystal structure. Broad diffraction pattern at 22.4° shows the characterized peak of carbon of nCDs. It was not significantly observed in the nCDs/Gd(OH)3 composite, and the intensity was also low; the broadness of carbon confirms the lattice disorder and distortion, small size, and amorphous nature [16]. When nCDs are composited with the metal hydroxide, they express the partial crystalline in nature. The characterized peak at 2θ = 16.2°, 28.5°, and 29.7° was assigned to the hexagonal phase of (100), (110), and (101) planes [17]. The most noticeable peak of Gd(OH)3, at 50.44, amply demonstrates the production of a structure resembling nano-rice. The HRTEM showed the same pattern of nano-spikes that helps in the chemical transformations [18]. The XRD data provide strong support for the nCDs/Gd(OH)3 hypothesis.

The Raman spectra clearly reveal the disordered and defects in the surface of nCDs/Gd(OH)3. The D band at 1340 cm−1 confirms the induced disorders in the CDs, and the G band at 1610 cm−1 reveals the graphitisation nature. The calculated ID/IG was around 1.5, proving the existence of disorders and defects in the material and the level of graphitization. The preponderance of surface functional groups, especially those of oxygenated and nitrogenated moieties, is the root reason. The introduction of heteroatom doping and RE elements has a significant impact on the formation of disorders. [19,20]

XPS was used to know the binding interaction and the surface oxidation state of the sample nCDs/Gd(OH)3. Fig. 3(a) clearly evidenced the elemental composition of nCDs/Gd(OH)3 that reveals the presence of 66 % carbon, 25 % oxygen, 8 % nitrogen, and 1 % gadolinium [21]. The deconvoluted C1s spectra display a prominent peak at 284.4 eV that indicates the presence of graphitic sp2 C=C/C-H as well as other noteworthy peaks belonging to the CN at 285.4 eV, CO, CN, C=O-N functional groups at 286.4 eV, 287.6 eV and 288.3 eV [22–24]. N 1 s spectra peaks at 398.6 eV and 399.5 eV reveal the presence of six-membered pyridinic N and Amino N (NH). 400.1 eV and 401.1 eV in the N 1 s spectra show that the produced materials include five-membered ring pyrrolic N and graphitic N(CN) enhancing adsorption sites [25–28]. The different forms of nitrogen help provide more catalytic active sites, mainly pyrrolic nitrogen, and graphitic nitrogen plays a vital role due to electron/hole transfer capability, which is its lowest barrier capability and significant selectivity towards OER. Vacancy defects are caused by the incorporation of nitrogen. Then, the presence of pyridinic nitrogen at the edge and the graphitized structure will coordinate with the rare earth metal ion for better stability and kinetics. A high level of N-doping in the CDs was confirmed, which boosted the performance [29]. The broad deconvoluted O 1 s peak indicates the presence of oxygen in multiple environments at 530.2 eV exhibits metal‑oxygen bond, 531.2 and 532.2 eV linked to the CO, CO groups [30] and 533.1 eV clearly states H-O-H groups [31–34]. The presence of gadolinium in the 3+ oxidation state is clearly evidenced by the 4d spectra of Gd(OH)3 at 141.4 eV and 146.6 eV [35,36]. A highly stable structure of nCDs/Gd(OH)3 resulting from the Gd3+ and nCDs has excellent coordination as per the earlier studies [37]. Overall, the nitrogen-doped carbon with rich surface functional groups and numerous vacancy defects enclosed in the Gd(OH)3 is the structural advantage for enhancing OER. For the detailed analysis of functional groups, the FTIR studies were carried out, as shown in Fig. 2(b).

The presence of hydroxyl group and NH stretching vibrations of nCDs, nCDs/Gd(OH)3 was confirmed by the presence of a peak between 3200 and 3400 cm−1 and a pointy tip at wavenumber 3614 cm−1 for Gd(OH)3 acknowledged by the existence of OH [38,39]. C-H(-CH2) stretching bonds are responsible for the production of absorption peaks at 2127 cm−1 [40]. Acute bands near 1638 cm−1 explicitly state the (−CO-NH) amide bending and CC stretching vibrations in the nCDs. In the composite, it was a little bit offset to 1608 cm−1 [41,42]. Peaks present near 1409 cm−1 and 1304 cm−1 are responsible for the carboxylate anions stretching vibrations and CN stretching mode [43,44]. The CO distinctive symmetry stretching band appears at 1073 cm−1 and a slight bending peak at 1008 cm−1 [45]. Moreover, the peak at 627 and 540 cm−1 was related to the stretching and vibration mode of M-O in the GdO, and the peak around 700 in Gd(OH)3 ascribed to OH bending vibrations and shifted to 780 cm−1 in the composite [46,47]. This conveys and confirms that nCDs and rare earth metal interactions are not solely caused by physical absorption but also by significant electrostatic interactions.

Using a three-electrode system, the behaviour of nCDs/Gd(OH)3 towards OER was investigated in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte. The graph shown in Fig. 4(a) shows that the prepared nCDs/Gd(OH)3 exhibits higher catalytic activity with the electrokinetic parameter of 280 mV overpotential at 10 mA/cm2. It is evident from the overpotential, which is 220 mV less than nCDs (500 mV). The sample's catalytic activity and charge transfer have improved due to the increased active sites. Similar measurements were taken for the comparison of uncoated GCE. The critical component in defining a material's electrocatalytic activity is its Tafel slope, and it has been observed that materials with lower Tafel slopes exhibit better kinetics for oxygen evolution. The calculated slope values of bare GCE, nCDs, and nCDs/Gd(OH)3 were 287, 204, and 127 mV/dec, respectively. The lowest value of nCDs/Gd(OH)3 betrays the faster kinetic rate of OER, which is evidence of the speedy charge transfer process. The stability of the material was proved by using the chronopotentiometry technique, which revealed a negligible change from 1.2613 to 1.2611, running at 10 mA/cm2 for 24 h. This possesses endurance, better durability nature, and a high level of activity in the alkaline environment.

EIS is a crucial tool to investigate the material's kinetic characteristics and coating impact. The impedance study was conducted for bare GCE, nCDs, and nCDs/Gd(OH)3 at 1 V, and the total impedance was calculated with the fitting circuit as 4.52 × 104 Ω, 2.8582 × 104 Ω, 0.052 × 103 Ω respectively. The entire resistance of the fitted circuit was supplied by solution, coating, and charge transfer, and the frequency response of the uncoated electrode is shown in Fig. S7. The increased OER activities were further evidenced and boosted by observation of the reduced overall resistance of nCDs/Gd(OH)3, which demonstrated a faster electron transfer from nCDs to Gd(OH)3 on the composite's surface.

It is well known that the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) was the main driver of electrocatalytic performance and that ECSA was completely reliant on the double-layer capacitance (Cdl). Using cyclic voltammetry for the potential window range between (0 V - 0.8 V) at several scan rates, ECSA was finally determined. CV graphs and the linear fit of current density are shown in Fig. 5 . The computed Cdl of nCDs/Gd(OH)3 is 0.062 mF/cm2, and according to previous investigations [48], the specific capacitance of the synthesized catalyst in 0.1 M KOH was 40 μF/cm2. The composite nCDs/Gd(OH)3 exhibits ECSA of 1.55 cm2, which is the fundamental reason for the better electron transfer, decreased Rct and enhanced OER activities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergetic impact of nCDs incorporated with Gd(OH)3 nanorods
ce:section.ce:para:
The synergistic effect of 1D nanorods and 0D dots plays a peculiar role in accelerating the electron transfer due to the decrease in recombination attributed to the electron lifetime by the nCDs/Gd(OH)3. The electron lifetime was calculated from the Bode plot derived from the EIS data with the help of the following formula,
(6)
τ n
=
1
2 π
f max
where ‘
τ n
’ denotes the lifetime of the electron and ‘
f max
’denotes the peak frequency in the low-frequency region, which also represents a transfer of electrons process happening at the working electrode and electrolyte interface. The estimated lifetime of electrons for the nCDs and nCDs/Gd(OH)3 was 1.30 ms and 4.15 ms, which resulted in a number of trapping active sites. This provides more ECSA and improved catalytic activity [49]. The base was set up for this excellent performance by incorporating nanorods with the amorphous carbon dots.

The introduction of a longitudinal structure regulates the electronic structure of the catalyst, and electrons can be easily channelized, which, in turn, favors the OER activity by reducing the electron-hole pair. This has been investigated by the XPS and reveals the change of nearly 1 eV in the binding energy, which provides the redistribution of charge at the nearby Gd(OH)3 and shows the electron transfer from nCDs to Gd(OH)3. The intense contact of nanorods with the carbon dot's functional moieties provides a robust platform for charge separation and transfer of electrons. This can also be proved by determining the surface electronic charge using Zeta potential, which shows potential moving towards the positive quadrant. It clearly states that the attraction of ions is getting enhanced, which is shown in Fig. S6 in the supplementary material [50]. Using scanning Kelvin probe microscopy (SKP), contact potential difference mapping was performed in this study to evaluate the average surface work function (WF) of nCDs. Fig. 6(c) of raster scan images reveals the average value of nCDs surface work function as 4.95 eV, which agrees with our past observations. The WF of Gd(OH)3 and nCDs/Gd(OH)3 were found to be 5.217 eV and 5.254 eV, respectively. The above values indicate that midstates exist that will promote hole transport from the nCDs to Gd(OH)3, which enhances the oxidation reaction. Based on the results, the graphical abstract is shown for nCDs/Gd(OH)3 for OER in the supplementary section as S1.

In order to study the formation of midstates and different energy levels, the electronic structure and energy band locations of the nCDs/Gd(OH)3 are revealed by SKP and UV–visible spectroscopy. The energy of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) state and similar to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) state were calculated using cyclic voltammetry of nCDs with the following equation.
(7)
E LUMO
= − e
E red
+ 4.4
(8)
E HOMO
= − e
E ox
+ 4.4

The reduction potentials of nCDs were observed as −0.266 V, and the Eg was calculated using a Tauc plot from UV–visible spectra data, which was shown in supplementary (Fig. S5 and S8). The E g values of nCDs and Gd(OH)3 were 5.56 and 5.40 eV, respectively, which matches earlier reports. [51]. The EHomo and ELumo of nCDs were measured as −4.134 and − 9.694 eV, respectively. The average surface work function of nCDs determined using the SKP system was 4.95 eV. The Gd(OH)3 nanorods conduction band edge was calculated using the following equation.
(9)
E CB
= X −
E c
−
1 2
E g
where ECB was determined to be −1.581 eV, and the corresponding valance band edge energy is 3.879 eV. The work function of nCDs and Gd(OH)3 was found to be 4.95 eV (Ef1) and 5.215 eV(Ef2). The different energy levels (shown in Fig. 7 ) proves the existence of midstates that will improve hole transport from the nCDs to Gd(OH)3, which enhances the OER.

Though nCDs play an influential role when compared to plain GCE because of their robust properties like quantum size, better conductivity, and rapidity of electron transfer, primarily, the quantum size provides a very high surface-to-volume ratio. The functional groups are rich in the surface and support the electrostatic interaction between nCDs and Gd(OH)3, which improves electrocatalytic activity. Integrating rare-earth metal nanorods with the green synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots flourishes energy production. According to Table T1 below, the potency of nCDs/Gd(OH)3 in OER characteristics was evaluated compared to other works.

Novel nCDs/Gd(OH)3 catalyst has been synthesized by reflux method using green synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots from Bhringraj leaves extract and gadolinium hydroxide by hydrothermal method. The presence of thrived functional groups on the surface of carbon dots because of heteroatom doping has been investigated using XPS and FTIR, which also describe the electrostatic interaction. The OER performance was examined in an alkaline medium where the overpotential of nCDs/Gd(OH)3 was 0.28 V(RHE), and the Tafel slope was 127 mV/dec, both of which were significantly less than that of nCDs and uncoated GCE. The effect of the insertion of gadolinium nanorods clearly evidenced that charge transfer resistance was dramatically reduced from the impedance data, and it also shows the time-constant studies as proof of the delay in recombination. Activity factors like time constant, work function, and surface electronic charge exhibit significant changes that reveal the incorporation of rare earth nanorods, which modify the nature of the catalyst. There are more opportunities for trapping energy because of the diverse energy levels that the morphology of composite production itself offers. This present study shows the enhancement of electrocatalytic activity in an alkaline medium with the help of rare earth metal, a combination of 1D and 0D material, and this can be evidenced by the chemical shift in the C 1 s state's binding energy with the value of 1 eV. This work encourages research in green energy production based on rare earth metals.

Nithya Selvaraju: Writing – original draft, Software, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Sheethal Sasi: Validation, Resources, Data curation. Yuvaraj Sivalingam: Validation, Resources, Data curation. Gunasekaran Venugopal: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very thankful to the Central University of Tamil Nadu, India, for providing an infrastructural, institutional research facility for this project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Schematic representation, Graphical Abstract, FESEM image of nanorods, UV – Vis Spectra, Tauc plot, Histogram of particle Size, Zeta potential studies, and Electrochemical studies of Bare GCE were reported. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111362.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrocatalytic study of carbon dots/nickel iron layered double hydroxide composite for oxygen evolution reaction in alkaline medium

Gopalakrishnan Sethuraman, Electrocatalytic study of carbon dots/Nickel iron layered double hydroxide composite for oxygen evolution reaction in alkaline medium, Fuel. 320 (2022) 123947. doi:10.1016/J.FUEL.2022.123947.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)/Co3O4 composite material as an efficient bi-functional electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution and oxygen reduction reactions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Muthurasu, S. V. Sheen Mers, V. Ganesh, Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)/Co3O4 composite material as an efficient bi-functional electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution and oxygen reduction reactions, Int. J. Hydrogen Energy. 43 (2018) 4726–4737. doi:10.1016/j.ijhydene.2017.11.157.
